title: The 'while' Loop ⏳
description: Keep repeating an action as long as a condition is true.

slides.title: When You Don't Know How Many Times 🤷‍♂️
slides.content:
# The `while` Loop

The `for` loop is great when you know you need exactly 10 iterations.

But what if you don't know? 
* "Keep asking the user for a password until they get it right."
* "Make it rain in the game while the storm cloud is active."

In these cases, we use a **`while`** loop. It reads just like English: "While THIS is true, DO that."

```javascript
while (condition) {
  // Code to run
}
```

slides.title: While Loop Structure 💻
slides.content: Unlike the `for` loop, a `while` loop only has a condition in its parentheses. You must manage any counter variables yourself!

quizData.question: What does a while loop put inside its parentheses `()`?

options.text: Just the condition to evaluate as true/false

quizData.explanation: A while loop is much simpler than a for loop. It only takes a single condition inside the (), running its code block repeatedly as long as that condition remains Truthy.

slides.title: For vs While 🤔

quizData.question: Why would you choose a while loop over a for loop?

options.text: When you know precisely how many times it needs to iterate
When the loop's end depends on an unknown event (like a user clicking a button or guessing a password)
When you want to prevent infinite loops

quizData.explanation: The while loop shines when the number of repetitions is unknown and based on a condition naturally changing over time (like game state or user input).

slides.title: A Word of Caution ⚠️
slides.content:
Because the `while` loop does not force you to provide a `step` argument like the `for` loop does, it is MUCH easier to accidentally write an infinite loop.

Always double-check that something *inside* your `while`'s code block will eventually cause the condition to become `false`!
